coredata.dc:title: The function of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from peapod biomass in composite photocatalysts for the enhanced photodegradation of perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) under UVC and visible light irradiation

coredata.dc:description: The remediation of perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) presents a significant challenge due to their environmental persistence and detrimental impact. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were sustainably prepared using peapod biomass via the hydrothermal method and incorporated into a titanium dioxide (TiO2) composite photocatalyst. Chemical, morphological, and optical analysis confirmed the efficient synthesis of the CQD/TiO2 composite, which exhibited enhanced optical properties. Photocatalytic oxidation experiments demonstrated that the CQD/TiO2 composite achieved significantly higher degradation efficiencies for perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA-C8) compared to pristine TiO2, with 26.3 % and 24.5 % improvements when irradiated under Ultraviolet-C (UVC) and visible light, respectively. Furthermore, the photocatalytic oxidation of short-chain PFCAs, specifically perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA-C6), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA-C5), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA-C4), and perfluoropropanoic acid (PFPrA-C3), were evaluated under visible light irradiation. Degradation rates for PFHxA-C6 and PFPeA-C5 significantly enhanced from 14.6 % and 19.6 % to 41.3 % and 52.1 %, respectively, when using CQD-coupled TiO2 (CQD/TiO2) compared to pure TiO2. The first-order rate constants revealed accelerated PFCA degradation using PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalysts. Intermediate formation (C3–C6) and defluorination analyses revealed stepwise decomposition of PFCAs, with enhanced CF₂ detachment in the presence of the CQD/TiO2 composite. The role of scavengers in PFOA degradation under visible light was also examined. This research underscores the potential of CQD-enhanced TiO2 photocatalysts for effective PFCA remediation under sustainable conditions.

head.ce:title: The function of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from peapod biomass in composite photocatalysts for the enhanced photodegradation of perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) under UVC and visible light irradiation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The remediation of perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) presents a significant challenge due to their environmental persistence and detrimental impact. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were sustainably prepared using peapod biomass via the hydrothermal method and incorporated into a titanium dioxide (TiO2) composite photocatalyst. Chemical, morphological, and optical analysis confirmed the efficient synthesis of the CQD/TiO2 composite, which exhibited enhanced optical properties. Photocatalytic oxidation experiments demonstrated that the CQD/TiO2 composite achieved significantly higher degradation efficiencies for perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA-C8) compared to pristine TiO2, with 26.3 % and 24.5 % improvements when irradiated under Ultraviolet-C (UVC) and visible light, respectively. Furthermore, the photocatalytic oxidation of short-chain PFCAs, specifically perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA-C6), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA-C5), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA-C4), and perfluoropropanoic acid (PFPrA-C3), were evaluated under visible light irradiation. Degradation rates for PFHxA-C6 and PFPeA-C5 significantly enhanced from 14.6 % and 19.6 % to 41.3 % and 52.1 %, respectively, when using CQD-coupled TiO2 (CQD/TiO2) compared to pure TiO2. The first-order rate constants revealed accelerated PFCA degradation using PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalysts. Intermediate formation (C3–C6) and defluorination analyses revealed stepwise decomposition of PFCAs, with enhanced CF₂ detachment in the presence of the CQD/TiO2 composite. The role of scavengers in PFOA degradation under visible light was also examined. This research underscores the potential of CQD-enhanced TiO2 photocatalysts for effective PFCA remediation under sustainable conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are synthetic chemicals characterized by substituting hydrogen atoms with fluorine atoms in a carbon framework, along with a terminal functional group. This unique structure creates a compact layer of trifluoromethyl groups, leading to water and oil repellency and amphiphilic characteristics with low molecular polarity, and high CF bond strength (536 kJ/mol), resulting in biological resistance [1]. These distinctive characteristics have driven the extensive use of PFAS across a wide range of industries globally, including packaging, pesticide products, water-resistant fabrics and carpets, non-stick cookware, smoke suppressants, firefighting foams, and various other applications. [2]. The widespread application of PFAS, particularly perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) as a subgroup, has led to significant contamination of groundwater and drinking water reservoirs. Due to their toxic properties, bioaccumulative tendencies, and persistent nature, regulatory restrictions on using long-chain PFAS in industrial applications have been progressively introduced. While the use of PFAS has been limited in many developed countries, these substances continue to be detected in water sources globally. Furthermore, short-chain PFAS are increasingly being used as substitutes. However, short-chain PFAS pose additional health and environmental risks due to their higher bond energy and greater water solubility [3–5].

The strong chemical stability of the CF bond in PFAS makes conventional water treatment methods, such as thermal and chemical processes, largely ineffective. To overcome these limitations, innovative technologies have been explored, including ultrasonic pyrolysis [6], adsorption treatments [7], electrochemical oxidation [3], membrane filtration [1], ion exchange [8], plasma treatments [9], electron beams [10], and gamma irradiation [2,11]. However, these methods face significant challenges that hinder their widespread application. These challenges include technological immaturity, the need for secondary recovery of byproducts, high energy requirements, elevated costs, slow degradation rates, difficulties in scaling up, and concerns about sustainability [12]. However, heterogeneous photocatalysis has emerged as a highly promising solution. It offers high efficiency, low cost, and environmental friendliness, utilizing sunlight as a free and renewable energy source [13,14].

As mentioned earlier, the strong CF bonding energy renders direct photolysis an ineffective method for removing PFAS from water [15]. However, using photocatalysts capable of absorbing photons (hv) facilitates the decomposition of PFAS across a broad range of light wavelengths. Semiconductor photocatalysts such as titanium dioxide (TiO2) [16], gallium(III) oxide (Ga2O3) [17], indium(III) oxide (In2O3) [18], and zinc oxide (ZnO) [19] have been extensively studied for their efficacy in degrading PFAS, particularly PFCAs. Among these, TiO2 is one of the most prominent and effective candidates due to its wide availability, affordability, chemical stability, significant redox potential, and non-toxic nature [20,21]. Despite its advantages, TiO2's photocatalytic performance is limited by several factors, including the rapid recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, a large band gap that hinders the absorption of visible light, and its restricted utility within the visible light spectrum. Surface engineering and the incorporation of nanostructured materials have been widely employed to address the limitations of TiO2 [20,22,23]. Among these strategies, using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as modifiers has proven particularly effective in significantly improving the photocatalytic degradation of emerging pollutants. This enhancement is achieved by optimizing the photocatalyst's surface morphology (including nanoparticle size and shape), expanding the light response range by reducing band gap energy, increasing the active surface site area, and facilitating efficient electron-hole separation and migration [22,24–26].

The pea (Pisum sativum L.) is the second most extensively cultivated legume worldwide and is among the oldest domesticated crops. Global statistics show that pea production reached 11.7 million tons in 2023 [27]. In Turkey, peas remain one of the most widely cultivated legumes, with an annual production of 3798 tons in the same year, showing a rising trend [28,29]. The outer pod comprises approximately 35–40 % of the pea's total weight. Significant quantities of pea residues are generated globally, with a substantial portion utilized as animal feed. They are characterized by their high protein content, ease of cultivation, cost-effectiveness, and widespread distribution across various regions. Given this substantial production, using peapod biomass as a sustainable waste resource is very important. In this study, peapod-derived CQDs were synthesized and applied using a photocatalytic oxidation approach for wastewater treatment. This not only promotes effective waste management but also introduces a green, sustainable method using an abundant carbon source for environmental remediation.

In the literature, various nanoparticles (NPs), including silver (Ag) [30,31], copper (Cu) [32], lead (Pb) [33], palladium (Pd) [31], platinum (Pt) [31], bismuth bromide oxide (BiOBr) [34], multi-walled carbon nanotubes [35], graphene oxide (GO) [16], and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [36], have been employed to modify TiO2 photocatalysts, significantly improving the photodegradation of PFOA—a persistent and concerning PFCA contaminant in water sources worldwide. Chen et al. achieved nearly 100 % PFOA degradation using Pb-modified TiO2 under a 400 W UV lamp (254 nm) [33]. Similarly, Chen et al. [34] reported 99.5 % degradation within 100 min with a BiOBr/TiO₂ composite under an Xe lamp with 300 W power and wavelengths between 320 and 780 nm. Gomez-Ruiz et al. [36] demonstrated 93 degradation over 12 h using rGO/TiO2 under a UV mercury lamp (200–600 nm). Li et al. [31] achieved 57.7 %, 94.2 %, and 100 % degradation efficiencies using Ag/TiO2, Pd–TiO2, and Pt–TiO2, respectively, under a 125 W mercury lamp (365 nm). A summary table comparing the efficiencies of various TiO2 modifications with NPs for the degradation of PFCAs is provided in Table S1. Although these studies demonstrate notable PFOA degradation efficiencies, significant challenges persist. The reliance on high-power Xenon and mercury lamps presents drawbacks such as high energy consumption, operational inefficiencies, and environmental concerns due to potential toxicity. In contrast, the LED lamps used in this study provide multiple benefits, such as reduced costs, enhanced energy efficiency, extended lifespans, and minimized environmental impact, making them a more sustainable alternative [37]. Furthermore, using certain nanoparticles, such as Pb and Pt, raises toxicity concerns [38]. At the same time, the high cost of noble metals like Ag and Pt poses limitations to scalability and broader adoption for environmental remediation. In recent studies, our group was the first to investigate the photocatalytic oxidation of PFOA and short-chain PFCAs using CQD-modified TiO2 [39]. These CQDs were derived from two sources: electrochemically exfoliated graphite nanorods and biomass-based mussel shells. While CQDs synthesized via exfoliated graphite significantly enhanced PFCA photocatalytic oxidation efficiency, mussel shell-based CQDs did not outperform pristine TiO2. Here, in this research, we demonstrate the successful incorporation of peapod biomass-based CQDs on TiO2, achieving enhanced photo efficiencies for PFOA as well as short-chain PFCAs. These results highlight the potential of green, sustainable approaches for water treatment and open new avenues for environmentally friendly remediation technologies.

Titanium (IV) butoxide (Ti(OBu)₄), sodium chloride (NaCl), acetic acid (CH₃COOH), ethanol (C₂H₅OH), methanol (CH₃OH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), ultra-high purity chromatography-grade water, and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA, C8), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA, C6), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA, C5), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA, C4), and perfluoropropanoic acid (PFPrA, C3) were all acquired from MERCK company. All chemicals utilized in this study were of analytical grade and were employed as received, without undergoing any additional purification processes. Ultra-pure water was obtained from Ege University's central water distillation system. Peapods used in the synthesis were purchased from a local grocery store.

PPCQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Fresh peapods were totally washed with ultrapure water and dried in an oven at 70 °C. Dried peapods were blended using a household blender and sieved to obtain a fine powder with particle sizes less than 150 μm. A total of 2 g of achieved powder was mixed with ultrapure water (40 mL) and ultrasonicated for 10 min to ensure homogeneity, with final magnetic stirring for 15 min. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon/PPL-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in the oven for 240 min at 190 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the solution containing PPCQDs was centrifuged for 30 min at 9000 rpm rotational speed. The upper clear solution was then filtered via a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove unreacted biomass. For further purification, the solution was dialyzed (1000 MW cutoff dialysis bag) for 1 day. The purified PPCQD solution was subsequently frozen and lyophilized to achieve dried CQDs. The synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 composite
ce:section.ce:para: Pure TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 were synthesized using the sol-gel method [40]. Initially, 10 mL of titanium (IV) butoxide was added to 40 mL of ethanol under magnetic stirring, and the mixture was stirred for an additional 30 min. Then, for the PPCQD/TiO2 composite, 2 mL of PPCQD solution (2 mg/mL) was added, while 2 mL of ultrapure water was used to synthesize bare TiO2. The mixture was stirred for another 30 min to ensure homogeneity. Next, the resulting solution was slowly added dropwise into a pre-prepared solution of 40 mL ethanol, 1 mL ultra pure water, and 4 mL acetic acid, which was vigorously stirred throughout the process. A colloidal suspension was prepared, stirred for an additional 6 h, and then aged for 60 h to facilitate the formation of a gel structure. The synthesized catalysts (TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2) were dried at 80 °C for 48 h and calcined at 500 °C for 3 h to achieve the final material.

To study the functional groups of the synthesized materials and the CQDs-TiO2 interaction, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, PerkinElmer Spectrum100) spectra were recorded with at a resolution of 4 cm−1 in between 650 cm−1 and 4000 cm−1 wavelength range. The crystal structure was recorded by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Panalytical Empyrean) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) in 2θ range of 10–80 ֠ with 2°.min−1 scan rate. The chemical state of the samples was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) with monochromatic Al Kα radiation (100–4000 eV). TEM images of CQDs were taken from Hitachi HT7800 electron microscope at a 100 kV accelerating voltage in high-resolution mode. The morphology and nanostructure of TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 were studied by collecting images using Aberration Corrected HRTEM (AC-HR-TEM) on a FEI Titan3 G2 equipped with double Cs-correctors at 300Kv. HRTEM samples were prepared on lacey carbon copper TEM grids by a drop-casting method. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a TA Instruments SDT Q600 system, with samples placed in alumina crucibles under a nitrogen atmosphere. The measurements were conducted with a nitrogen flow rate of 100 mL.min−1 and a heating rate of 10 °C.min−1 up to a maximum temperature of 600 °C.

CQDs absorption properties were characterized by UV–Vis (GENESIS 10S, Thermo Scientific) spectrometer with a wavelength ranging from 200 nm to 400 nm. The photoluminescence emission (PL) spectrum was measured via a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FS5). UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded by a Shimadzu 2600 spectrophotometer. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted using an Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (AUT 204 S) equipped with a frequency response analyzer (FRA) module, covering a frequency range from 10−3 Hz to 106 Hz. A conventional three-electrode system was used, involving a photocatalyst-coated FTO electrode as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire as the counter electrode. Chronoamperometry measurements for photocurrent response and intensity-modulated voltammetry spectroscopy (IMVS) were performed using the Autolab Optical Bench kit. Photocurrent response measurements were performed under intermittent 470 nm (blue) LED light irradiation, with 50-s on/off intervals. For the IMVS measurements, the AC amplitude was set to 10 % of the light intensity (470 nm), with a frequency range between 20 kHz and 100 mHz, capturing ten data points per decade.

The concentration of PFCAs was measured using liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS, Shimadzu LCMS-8040). Measurements were carried out in negative electrospray ionization (ESI-) mode with XDB-C18 column (2.1 mm × 150 mm, 5 μm) under 300 μL/min flow rate and 10 mL injection volume for chromatographic separation. During the measurements, an electrospray ionization capillary voltage of −3.5 kV was applied, while the desolvation and heat block temperatures were controlled at 250 °C and 400 °C, respectively. The mobile phases employed for the analysis were designated as Solvent A and Solvent B. Solvent A was comprised of 20 mM ammonium acetate in a mixture composed of 5 % methanol and 95 % ultrapure water, while Solvent B consisted solely of 100 % methanol. Initially, the mobile phase composition was set to 90 % A and 10 % B for the first 3 min. A gradient elution was then applied, with Solvent B held at 100 % from 3 to 6.7 min, followed by a linear decrease from 100 % to 10 % B between 6.7 and 9 min.

An ion chromatograph (IC, Thermo Scientific Dionex ICS-5000 DC) provided with an autosampler and a Dionex IonPac AS19 analytical column (4 × 250 mm) was used to analyze the fluoride ion (F−) concentration after the photocatalytic oxidation process. Ultrapure water-based KOH solutions were used as the mobile phase at a flow rate of 1 mL/min. The gradient program consisted of 12 mM KOH for the first 0–10 min, 45 mM KOH for 10–30 min, and a return to 12 mM KOH for the final 0.1 min. Defluorination rates were determined using Eq. (1):

Photocatalytic oxidation of PFOA and a mixture of short-chain PFCAs (PFHxA, PFPeA, PFBA, and PFPrA, (C6–C3) was conducted under irradiation of UVC (100–280 nm) and visible light (400–700 nm) in the experimental setup (Fig. S1). Bare TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 composite (both at 1 g/L) were used as photocatalysts in a cylindrical batch quartz reactor with a volume of 150 mL. PFOA solution or mixture of short-chain PFCAs (both 100 mL), each with an initial concentration of 100 μg/L, was added to the reactor for irradiation. To study the role of reactive species, scavengers were introduced separately at 1 mM concentrations, including ammonium oxalate (AO) as a hole (h+) scavenger, benzoquinone (BQ) as a superoxide (O₂−·) scavenger, and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) as a hydroxyl radical (OH·) scavenger. To ensure adsorption-desorption equilibrium, the solution was stirred in the dark for 30 min before light exposure. The PFCAs in the reactor were irradiated for 480 min using either four UVC lamps (16 W, 1.36 W/cm2, 254 nm) or an array of 112 visible light LEDs (200 W, 0.42 W/cm2, 420–700 nm), as illustrated in Fig. S1. Samples were collected at 30, 60, 180, 240, 360, 420, and 480-min intervals for subsequent analysis.

FTIR analysis investigated the chemical composition and bonding characteristics of PPCQD, TiO2, and the PPCQD/TiO2 composite (Fig. 2 (a)). This characterization aims to confirm the successful synthesis and integration of PPCQD with TiO2 and to identify any new chemical bonds that form between them, which could influence the material's properties. In the PPCQD spectrum, transmittance peaks at approximately 3277 cm−1 and 2935 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of N-H/O-H and CH bonds, respectively [41]. Additionally, peaks at 1648 cm−1, 1578 cm−1, 1400 cm−1, 1248 cm−1, and 1040 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrations of CO, CC, CN, C-O-C, and CO bonds [42,43]. Fainter peaks within the range of 675–900 cm−1 arise from CH bonds bending vibrations [44].

The TiO2 synthesized using the sol-gel method was treated at 500 °C for 3 h, resulting in complete water content evaporation. Thus, no hydroxyl groups were observed in its FTIR spectra. The characteristic transmittance peak below 880 cm−1 signifies the presence of Ti-O-Ti bonds in the structure [22,45]. In the PPCQD/TiO2 spectrum, the broader transmittance peak below 886 cm−1 undergoes a peak broadening compared to TiO2, signifying a robust chemical connection between the PPCQD and TiO2 through the formation of Ti–O–C bonds [46,47]. Additionally, FTIR analysis was performed on the PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalyst composite after its application in the photodegradation of PFOA under visible light irradiation to investigate structural changes. The composite was thoroughly rinsed with ultrapure water and ethanol three times, then dried in an oven at 65 °C for 6 h, following the same procedure used during the reusability tests after filtration from the PFOA solution. The FTIR spectra of PPCQD/TiO2 before and after photodegradation are shown in Fig. S2. After use, the composite exhibited additional transmittance peaks at 1208 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1, corresponding to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of CF2 + CF3 and CF2 bonds from PFOA [48,49]. These findings indicate the presence of PFOA residues on the surface of the photocatalyst, which contributes to the decline in photodegradation efficiency observed in reusability tests. Therefore, additional purification steps are recommended to improve the performance of the photocatalyst in repeated applications.

To study the crystal structure of bare TiO2, and PPCQD/TiO2 composite XRD measurements were carried out (Fig. 2 (b)). In bare TiO2, distinct anatase phase (depicted as A in the XRD pattern) peaks at angles of 25.30 (101), 37.90 (004), 48.10 (200), 540 (105), 55.10 (211), 62.70 (204), 68.90 (116), 70.30 (220), and 75.10 (215) can be seen [46,47]. Furthermore, weak peaks at 27.40, 36.130, 41.30, and 56.60 correspond to the diffraction pattern of the rutile TiO2 (110), (101), (111) and (220) planes (depicted as R in the XRD pattern) [50,51]. Besides, using Eq. (2), the anatase phase percentage ratio (A %) was calculated for both bare TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 (Table 1 ), in which IR and IA in Eq. 2 are the rutile and anatase peaks intensities. The results imply that the crystal structure of TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 with 89.36 and 94.03 A% are predominantly composed of the anatase phase. At calcination temperatures above 800 °C, TiO2 NPs undergo a phase transformation from anatase and brookite to a pure rutile phase. Experimental and analytical studies have consistently demonstrated that anatase TiO2 exhibits superior photocatalytic activity compared to the rutile phase under UVC and visible light irradiation. As the calcination temperature increases, rutile-phase TiO2 NPs exhibit significant crystal growth, leading to larger particle sizes and reduced surface area. In contrast, anatase-phase TiO2 synthesized at lower calcination temperatures achieves complete crystallization with more petite, uniform, and spherical particles and a larger surface area—key factors that enhance its photocatalytic efficiency. Furthermore, anatase TiO2 is characterized by a higher concentration of oxygen vacancies compared to rutile, contributing to a longer charge-carrier lifetime, extended exciton diffusion length, and improved charge-carrier mobility, all of which are critical for its superior photocatalytic activity [52–55].

When comparing the XRD pattern of PPCQD/TiO2 with TiO2, a similar pattern can be detected without any characteristic peak of PPCQDs in the composite. This may be due to the high dispersion, low amount of PPCQDs, and relatively weak diffraction intensity, suggesting small to no effect of CQDs on the crystal structure of TiO2 [22].

Crystalline sizes of TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 were calculated using the Debye-Sherrer Eq. (3), and the results are given in Table 1. The crystalline size of TiO2 is 26.78 nm. In the diffraction pattern of PPCQD/TiO2, the calculated full width at half maximum (FWHM) at 25.30 diffraction peak is wider. Subsequently, the crystalline size of the PPCQD/TiO2 composite decreased slightly to 25.05 nm, showing that the PPCQDs hindered the crystal growth of TiO2 given in Table 1.

where k is Sherrer constant and equal to 0.9, λ denotes the wavelength of the x-ray beam (Cu Kα, with λ = 0.1540598 nm), β (in radians) represents the FWHM of the most intense peak (25.3°), while θ (in radians) denotes the Bragg angle presented in Table 1.

TGA measurements were performed on both pure TiO2 and the PPCQD-loaded TiO2 composite to quantify the CQD content and assess thermal behavior. As presented in Fig. 2 (c), pure TiO2 exhibits a weight loss of approximately 0.6 %, primarily attributed to the evaporation of adsorbed moisture. In contrast, the PPCQD/TiO2 composite shows a total weight loss of 1.3 %, corresponding to both moisture evaporation and the thermal decomposition of CQDs present on the TiO2 surface [56]. This analysis estimates the CQD content in the composite to be approximately 0.7 %.

XPS measurements were performed to analyze the elemental composition and chemical state of the materials of synthesized bare PPCQDs, TiO2, and PPCQD/TiO2 composite. The full survey spectra (Fig. 3 (a)) of PPCQDs displayed two distinct peaks representing carbon (C 1 s, 285.46 eV) and oxygen (O 1 s, 532.11 eV), with a lower proportion of nitrogen (N 1 s, 400.08 eV) [57]. The detailed O 1 s spectra (Fig. 3 (b)) demonstrate the existence of functional groups including CO, C-OH/C-O-C, CO, and O-H/C=O at 532.6, 532.34, 531.66, and 531.06 eV, respectively [57,58]. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 3 (d)) are attributed to the C=C/C-C (284.78 eV), C-O/C-OH/C-N (286 eV), and CO (287.58 eV) functional groups [57,59].

In the XPS survey scan of bare TiO2, and composite with PPCQDs incorporation (Fig. 3 (a)), significant peaks corresponding to Ti 2p, O 1 s, and C 1 s were identified at approximately 459, 530, and 285 eV, respectively. The detection of the C 1 s peak in bare TiO2 primarily suggests the occurrence of surface carbon contamination during synthesis, as documented in relevant research [60,61]. In Ti 2p spectra (Fig. 3 (c)), the peaks at 464.1 and 458.3 eV for TiO2, and 464.3 and 458.5 eV for PPCQD/TiO2 assigned to the characteristic Ti4+ of Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2, with 5.8 eV peak separation. These results agree well with XRD results, conforming to the tetragonal anatase phase TiO2 formation [47]. The observed shift toward higher binding energies of Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 in the composite is attributed to the electronic interaction between PPCQDs and TiO2. Specifically, the presence of PPCQDs facilitates electron transfer to TiO2, increasing the electron density around Ti centers and causing a small change in the binding energy, consistent with the formation of Ti–O–C bonds. This phenomenon aligns with previous studies, where CQD loading on TiO2 resulted in similar Ti 2p shifts due to changes in the local electronic environment. In addition, the variations in peak areas observed in the high-resolution spectra can be attributed to surface modifications induced by the PPCQDs. The electron-rich environment created by PPCQD loading leads to altered surface chemistry, where Ti species near the PPCQDs exhibit slightly different electronic surroundings compared to those in pure TiO2. This heterogeneity results in changes to the relative peak intensities and areas, reflecting differences in the chemical environment and possibly a slight increase in surface defects or oxygen vacancies due to the composite formation [45,62–64].

High-resolution O 1 s spectra are given in Fig. 3 (b). In TiO2, peaks at 531.3 and 529.64 eV are due to C-OH and TiO. In PPCQD/TiO2 spectra, similar functional groups can be seen but with a shift toward higher binding energies (531.41 eV and 529.73 eV), indicating electron redistribution at the TiO2 surface due to the formation of Ti–O–C linkages with PPCQDs. This also supports the hypothesis of electron transfer between TiO₂ and PPCQDs, creating an interfacial region with modified electronic characteristics [47,62,64]. In the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3 (d)), characteristic peaks for O=C-O, C-O/C-OH/C-N, and C-C/C=C functional groups appeared at 287.64, 286.63, and 284.71 eV for PPCQD/TiO2, confirming the existence of PPCQDs within the composites. When we compare the C1s spectra of pure PPCQD and composite, it could be said that there were redshifts in O=C-O and CO binding energies and noticeable alterations in peak intensity, indicative of an enhanced coupling effect [65,66].

The TEM image of PPCQDs and their corresponding size distribution histogram are presented in Fig. 4(a)–(b). The PPCQDs exhibit a well-defined spherical morphology with a uniform distribution across the imaged area. Gaussian fitting analysis (summarized in Table S2) indicates an average CQD size of 4.10 nm. To further investigate the structural properties and confirm the successful synthesis of TiO₂ and the doping of PPCQDs onto its surface, HRTEM images were obtained, and the interplanar spacings were measured, as shown in Fig. 4 (c)-(d). The measured interplanar spacing of 0.36 nm corresponds to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2, which is in agreement with the XRD results. Additionally, the 0.25 nm spacing observed in Fig. 4 (d) corresponds to the (100) plane lattice spacing of sp2 graphite carbon within the PPCQDs. These findings confirm that PPCQDs are homogeneously distributed on the TiO2 surface, supporting the successful integration of the CQDs into the composite material [67].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of PPCQDs were measured at room temperature to investigate their optical properties, as shown in Fig. S3. The absorption peak observed around 271 nm corresponds to the π–π* transition of the CC bonds, while the shoulder band around 329 nm is attributed to the n-π* transitions of the CO and CN bonds. These transitions are characteristic of conjugated systems and indicate the presence of key functional groups in the CQDs [68].

The fluorescent properties of the synthesized CQDs were also examined under 365 nm UVA light (inset, Fig. S3). Under daylight, the PPCQDs appear brown, but when exposed to UVA light, they exhibit a bright green fluorescence, indicating a relatively high quantum yield. This visual fluorescence provides further evidence of the successful synthesis and formation of the CQDs, confirming their desirable optical properties [69].

Photoluminescence (PL) refers to the emission of light from a material after it absorbs photons. The PL mechanism originates from transitions between energy states in the band gaps, often associated with π-conjugated domains (sp2-hybridized regions). In CQDs, PL is influenced by several factors, including surface defects, quantum size effects, the carbon core state, surface passivation or functionalization, and various emissive traps present on the CQD surface [70,71]. To explore the optical properties of PPCQDs, PL measurements were conducted in deionized (DI) water (0.1 mg/mL), as depicted in Fig. 5 (a). The emission spectra were collected at excitation wavelengths between 380 and 500 nm, with 10 nm intervals. The results showed the reduction of PL intensity and red shifting of the fluorescence emission peaks to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increased from 380 nm to 500 nm. Notably, the peak PL intensity was observed at an emission wavelength of 455 nm when the excitation wavelength was set to 380 nm. This excitation-dependent behavior is attributed to the distribution of different surface energy traps and the variation in CQD sizes. The red-shifting of the emission wavelength as surface oxidation increased suggests that the degree of surface oxidation significantly influences CQD fluorescence [70–72]. Additionally, to assess the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs of bare TiO2 and PPCQD-doped TiO2 photocatalysts, PL studies were carried out under 380 nm excitation, as shown in Fig. 5 (b). The results revealed that bare TiO2 exhibited significantly higher luminescence compared to the PPCQD/TiO2 composite. A lower PL intensity correlates with a reduced electron-hole recombination rate and more efficient separation of conduction band electrons and valance band holes, which is beneficial for photocatalysis. This reduction in PL intensity demonstrates that the incorporation of PPCQDs into TiO2 effectively decreases the recombination probability of electron-hole pairs, which may enhance the photocatalytic activity of the composite [22,73].

The diffuse reflectance spectra and the band gap energies of pure TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalysts were determined using UV–Vis DRS analysis given in Fig. 5 (c)-(d). These results give precise information about the visible light absorption capacity and energy needed to excite an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, which is important in the estimation of the photophysical, photochemical, and photocatalytic activity of semiconductors under visible light irradiation. Reflectance ratio results given in Fig. 5 (c) show about 55.2 % reflectance of TiO2 at visible light regions higher than 400 nm wavelength. By the incorporation of PPCQDs on the surface of the TiO2, the light absorbance capacity increases with a decrease in reflectance ratio to 48 %. These results prove that the introduction of CQDs enhances the photocatalytic activity of TiO2, in the visible light spectrum [74]. The band gap energy was calculated from Tauc plot

In the Tauc plot where n = 2 for an indirect band gap semiconductor, the intercept of the extrapolated tangent line with the horizontal axis gives the band gap energy [74]. To verify the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalyst composite, transient photocurrent response measurements were conducted using the chronoamperometry method. The tests were performed on bare PPCQD, TiO2, and the PPCQD/TiO2 composite over several light on-off cycles for 450 s (50 s light on, 50 s light off). As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the photocurrent values for all samples increased immediately when the light was switched on and returned to near zero once the light was turned off, demonstrating the typical behavior of photo-responsive materials [78].

Bare PPCQD exhibited superior transport of photo-generated electrons, greater efficiency in electron-hole separation, with enhanced amount of photoinduced charge carrier generation, resulting in the highest photocurrent intensity. As expected, the PPCQD-doped TiO2 composite showed a significantly higher photocurrent response compared to bare TiO2. This increase is attributed to the combined photocatalytic activity of the composite, which promotes faster charge transfer to the electron acceptor at the surface of the photocatalyst. This results in a more efficient separation of electron-hole pairs, thus enhancing the overall photocatalytic activity of the PPCQD/TiO2 composite [22,45,79].

To further investigate the charge-transfer properties of the TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalysts, EIS measurements were conducted. Nyquist plots, recorded at a bias voltage of 0.2 V, are presented in Fig. 6 (b). The semi-circular arcs in the plots provide key insights into the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) at the heterojunction interface, while the solution resistance (Rs), calculated based on the fitted equivalent circuit model (Fig. S4), reflects both resistance of the electrolyte solution and the coated FTO substrate [80,81]. Results given in Table 2 indicate that doping CQDs in PPCQD/TiO₂ composite effectively reduces the Nyquist plot radius and Rct and Rs as well. This reduction in the Nyquist plot radius for the PPCQD/TiO₂ composite, compared to pure TiO₂, further confirms the enhanced charge transfer efficiency at the heterojunction interface, thereby improving photocatalytic activity due to the incorporation of CQDs. PL, photocurrent response, and EIS analyses collectively demonstrate that incorporating PPCQDs into TiO2 effectively enhances both charge separation and charge transfer efficiency. These findings confirm that CQD decoration significantly improves the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under visible light irradiation by suppressing electron-hole recombination and promoting efficient charge transfer [50,82].

The time for electron recombination (TR) of the photocatalysts was analyzed using IMVS techniques using Eq. 5, which is effective for investigating the transport dynamics of photoinduced charge carriers.
(5)

1
2 π
f IMVS

Here, ƒIMVS represents the minimum frequency at the imaginary component of the IMVS Nyquist plots (Fig. 6 (c) and Table 2), respectively. The time for electron recombination (ƬR) denotes the lifetime of the majority of carriers under open-circuit conditions. Results show ƬR increases when PPCQDs were doped on the TiO2, indicating the electron recombination rate decrease in PPCQD/TiO2 (Table 2). The observed improvement suggests that the recombination of electrons in the TiO2 photocatalyst is effectively mitigated through the introduction of oxygen vacancies and the incorporation of oxygen vacancy-rich CQDs. These findings provide supportive evidence for the corresponding photocurrent results.

The photocatalytic oxidation of PFOA under UVC and visible light irradiation was investigated alongside a mixed solution of short-chain PFCAs, including PFHxA (C6), PFPeA (C5), PFBA (C4), and PFPrA (C3), under visible light irradiation. Both TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalysts were utilized, with results presented in Fig. 7 (a)–(d). The photodegradation efficiencies of PFOA using the TiO2 photocatalyst under UVC and visible light irradiation were 40.9 % and 23.7 %, respectively. However, incorporation of CQDs on the surface of TiO2 (PPCQD/TiO2) efficiently enhanced the photodegradation efficiency to 67.2 % under UVC and 48.5 % under visible light irradiation. These findings corroborate electrochemical and optical analyses, including PL, UV–Vis-DRS, EIS, photocurrent intensity, and IMVS, demonstrating that incorporating CQDs improves the photocatalyst's performance. CQDs facilitate electron transfer between the VB and CB of TiO2 and act as electron traps, effectively minimizing electron-hole recombination. Additionally, they reduce the band gap and enhance the light absorption capacity of the photocatalyst composite [16,33,36]. Consequently, the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 for PFCA photodegradation was noticeably improved by incorporating PPCQD, achieving 26.3 % and 24.8 % higher photodegradation efficiency for PFOA under UVC and visible light, respectively. It is essential to highlight that the UVC light intensity, measured at 1.36 W/cm2, was approximately six times higher than that of visible light (0.42 W/cm2), leading to significantly higher energy consumption. However, the photodegradation efficiency of PFOA under UVC irradiation was only 17.2 % and 18.7 % higher when using TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalysts, respectively, compared to visible light. Despite the much higher energy input, this relatively modest improvement underscores the potential advantages of visible light-driven processes. These findings suggest that visible light, with its lower energy demand and comparable degradation performance, offers a more sustainable and efficient pathway for the remediation of persistent pollutants such as PFCAs in water treatment.

Photodegradation efficiencies of short-chain PFCAs in a mixed solution using bare TiO2 (Fig. 7 (c)) were as follows: 19.6 % for PFHxA-C6, 14.6 % for PFPeA-C5, 26.9 % for PFBA-C4, and 24.2 % for PFPrA-C3. Interestingly, shorter-chain PFCAs such as PFBA-C4 and PFPrA-C3 exhibited slightly higher degradation efficiencies than their longer-chain counterparts PFHxA-C6 and PFPeA-C5 under visible light irradiation with pristine TiO2. This trend, which differs from the commonly reported lower degradability of shorter-chain PFCAs due to their higher CF bond strengths, higher solubility, and lower hydrophobicity, may result from diffusion and adsorption dynamics in the mixed solution [83]. Specifically, longer-chain PFCAs likely face reduced mass transfer and steric hindrance, limiting their adsorption onto the bare TiO2 surface. In contrast, the smaller size and higher mobility of short-chain PFCAs facilitate better surface contact [84]. Upon introducing the PPCQD/TiO2 composite, a significant enhancement in photodegradation efficiency was observed for the longer-chain PFCAs, with PFHxA-C6 and PFPeA-C5 achieving 41.3 % and 52.1 % degradation, respectively, compared to 14.6 % and 19.6 % with pristine TiO2 (Fig. 7 (d)). However, a notable decline was observed for the shorter-chain PFCAs, where PFBA-C4 and PFPrA-C3 degradation efficiencies decreased to 24.6 % and 17.2 %, respectively. This behavior can be rationalized by the altered surface characteristics of the PPCQD/TiO2 composite. The incorporation of PPCQDs increases the surface hydrophobicity and modifies the surface charge distribution, favoring stronger interactions with the more hydrophobic longer-chain PFCAs (C5–C8) through van der Waals forces and hydrophobic interactions [85]. Consequently, these longer-chain PFCAs outcompete shorter-chain PFCAs for active sites on the photocatalyst surface [86]. Additionally, the PPCQDs may enhance charge separation and electron transfer for longer-chain PFCA species due to their more favorable adsorption orientation and stronger surface binding [87]. In contrast, shorter-chain PFCAs, being more hydrophilic, exhibit weaker adsorption on the modified PPCQD/TiO2 surface, limiting their direct participation in photocatalytic degradation. This competitive adsorption effect, combined with variations in molecular size, surface affinity, and electron transfer dynamics, contributes to the observed shift in degradation trends [87].

These findings align with prior reports, including Hori et al. [88], who noted limited degradation of short-chain PFCAs under photocatalytic conditions, and Liu et al. [89], who reported negligible degradation for C1–C3 PFCAs. Our previous study [39] also highlighted the role of CQDs in enhancing longer-chain PFCA degradation through improved charge separation. In the present study, the green synthesis of PPCQDs from peapod biomass further promotes this effect, particularly improving the degradation of PFHxA-C6, PFPeA-C5, and PFOA-C8 while limiting short-chain PFCA degradation due to altered adsorption and surface interaction dynamics. Overall, this highlights the critical role of surface chemistry and competitive interactions in dictating photocatalytic selectivity across PFAS chain lengths [90].

The decomposition rate constants for pristine TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 in the photodegradation of PFCAs under visible and UVC light were calculated and summarized in Table S3. The results demonstrate that under visible light irradiation, the degradation of PFOA, PFHxA-C6, and PFPeA-C5 proceeded 3.1, 3.5, and 7.3 times faster, respectively, when PPCQD/TiO2 was employed compared to pristine TiO2. In contrast, the rate constants for PFBA-C4 and PFPrA-C3 remained nearly identical for both photocatalysts, likely due to the increased resistance of shorter-chain PFCAs to degradation. Under UVC irradiation, the decomposition of PFOA using PPCQD/TiO2 was approximately twice as fast as that achieved with pure TiO2, further confirming the performance enhancement provided by CQD modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation mechanism, intermediates formed during photodegradation, and defluorination efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
The possible mechanism for the photocatalytic performance of the PPCQD/TiO2 composite photocatalyst is described in Fig. 8 (a). As given in the optical properties of composite photocatalyst, the successful incorporation of PPCQD to the TiO2 structure extended the photoresponsive range of TiO2 from UV to the visible region. It reduced the band gap of TiO2 from 3.04 eV to 2.95 eV, thus improving the utilization efficiency of visible light and improving the photocatalytic activity of PPCQD/TiO2 composites. Here the role of PPCQD is very important because it acts as
e
CB
−
reservoirs and provides the electrons to the conduction band of TiO2 during photodegradation. These electrons trap the molecular oxygen to produce
O 2
∗ −
radicals. However,
O 2
∗ −
radical is not stable in an aqueous solution and can be rapidly converted to hydroxyl radicals. It is known that holes are a source of
OH ∗
radical during photodegradation.

Several studies in literature proved the excellent electrical conductivity property of CQDs, which are useful for the fast transfer of photoinduced electrons and act as electron sinks in the photocatalytic degradation of several organic pollutants. In the study done by Wang et al., 2024, incorporation of CQDs into the TiO2 enhanced the photodegradation of Rhodamine B under visible light. They explained this improvement by the synergistic interaction of enhancement of visible light absorption and separation efficiency of the photoinduced electron-hole pairs by the help of the oxygen vacancies and CQDs [91]. In another study conducted by Mozdbar et al., 2023, the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs/TiO2 was investigated for the removal of Methylene Blue under visible light, and they achieved 92.1 % photodegradation within 120 min which is 2.4 times higher than that of bare TiO2. They concluded that the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites under visible light was significantly affected by the electron transfer from carbon quantum dots to TiO2 nanoparticles [92]. In another study, the fabricated CQDs/TiO2 composites showed remarkably improved photodegradation performances of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible-light irradiation which was 43.7-fold higher than that of pure TiO2. This positive development was explained by the fact that the successful combination of TiO2 and CQDs can increase the visible light utilization efficiency and CQDs can act as e− reservoir and donor to accelerate the separation/transfer of photoinduced charge carriers and improve the photocatalytic performance by suppressing the photogenerated e− and h+ recombination [62]. Similarly, in the study done by Xu et al., 2023, CQDs were found to enhance photon absorption in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum and hence phenol degradation by promoting the separation of photogenerated charge carriers via electron transfer through Ti–O–C bonds formed at the TiO2-CQD interface [93].

The roles of formed
OH ∗
radicals in PFCA photodegradation may be explained in the following steps [33,36,62,94,95].

Although the photooxidation mechanism is mainly driven by OH⁎ radical attack, the superoxide anions
O 2 ∗
−
may also play a role to form perfluorocarboxylic acid with one fewer CF2 unit [36]. Consequently, these reactions proceed stepwise until the complete mineralization of PFCAs and the generation of short-chain intermediates. Fig. 8 (a) illustrates an example of the photodegradation pathway of PFOA, highlighting the decomposition process of PFCAs.

To gain a deeper understanding of the photodegradation pathways and underlying mechanisms of PFCAs, this study investigated intermediate products, including PFHxA-C6, PFPeA-C5, PFBA-C4, and PFPrA-C3 compounds, formed during the photodegradation of PFOA under UVC and visible light irradiation (Fig. 8 (b)-(c)). Defluorination results for PFOA and short-chain PFCAs (C3–C6) were also thoroughly examined (Fig. 8 (d)).

Under UVC irradiation (Fig. 8 (b)), shorter-chain PFCAs began forming at the onset of illumination. The highest concentrations of longer-chain intermediates, C6 (6.14 μg/L) and C5 (6.97 μg/L), were observed for up to 240 min. Beyond this point, the concentration of C6 and C5 decreased to 4.77 μg/L and 7.98 μg/L by the end of the reaction. The concentration of C3 and C4 increased linearly, reaching 7.32 μg/L and 10.17 μg/L, respectively, at 480 min. These results highlight the stepwise decomposition of PFCAs into shorter chains, consistent with literature-proposed mechanisms that involve CF₂ elimination. Moreover, the enhanced photodegradation efficiency of shorter-chain PFCAs (C3 and C4) when using TiO2 photocatalysts can be attributed to the progressive breakdown of C5 and C6 into shorter-chain products.

Under visible light irradiation (Fig. 8 (c)), the formation of intermediates C6 and C5 was evident from the beginning of the reaction. C6 reached a maximum concentration of 9.13 μg/L at 360 min before decreasing to 4.99 μg/L by the reaction's end. C5 showed a rapid increase within the first 30 min, followed by a slower accumulation, ultimately reaching 8.32 μg/L at 480 min. The formation of C3 and C4 began after 30 min of irradiation and increased continuously with highest concentrations of 5.77 μg/L and 6.92 μg/L, at the end of the reaction respectively. These findings further corroborate the stepwise degradation of longer-chain PFCAs into shorter chains under visible light, reinforcing the decomposition pathway observed under UVC irradiation.

Fig. 8 (d) presents the photocatalytic defluorination ratios of PFOA-C8 and short-chain PFCAs (C3–C6) in a mixed solution after 480 min of UVC and visible light irradiation using TiO2 and PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalysts. The findings reveal a significant enhancement in the defluorination ratio of 100 ppb PFOA under visible light irradiation, increasing from 11.6 % with bare TiO2 to 26.1 % with the PPCQD/TiO2 composite. Under UVC irradiation, the defluorination ratio improved from 23.2 % with TiO2 to 29.9 % with the PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalyst. The introduction of PPCQD onto TiO2 notably increased defluorination under both visible and UVC light, with a more pronounced enhancement under visible light. This demonstrates the effectiveness of PPCQD/TiO2 in harvesting visible light, supporting its potential for sustainable and efficient wastewater treatment. For short-chain PFCAs (C3–C6) in the mixed solution, the defluorination ratio with TiO2 was 4.2 % under visible light and 5.1 % under UVC irradiation. When PPCQD/TiO2 was used, these values increased to 5.7 % and 7.2 %, respectively, further confirming the superior photocatalytic activity of the modified composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of scavengers on photodegradation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The results shown in Fig. 9 reveal that the introduction of specific radical scavengers significantly suppressed the photodegradation efficiency of PFOA. Ammonium oxalate (AO) was used as a hole (h+) scavenger, benzoquinone (BQ) as a superoxide radical (O2 −•) scavenger, and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) as a hydroxyl radical (OH•) scavenger. The degradation efficiency decreased markedly from 48.5 % to 37.9 %, 24.1 %, and 20.9 % in the presence of AO, BQ, and IPA, respectively. These results highlight the critical role of hydroxyl radicals (OH•) in the degradation process, as their quenching led to the most substantial decline in efficiency. Additionally, both holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (O₂−•) also play important roles, though h+ appears to have the least contribution among the reactive species examined. Overall, these findings demonstrate that hydroxyl radicals, superoxide radicals, and photoinduced holes synergistically contribute to the effective degradation of PFOA using the PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalyst under visible light irradiation.

The enhanced photocatalytic performance of the PPCQD/TiO2 composite can be attributed to the role of CQDs in facilitating both charge separation and charge transfer processes. The reduction of the TiO2 band gap from 3.04 eV to 2.95 eV upon CQD incorporation, as confirmed by UV–Vis DRS, extends the light absorption into the visible region. Moreover, PL spectra exhibit lower emission intensity for the composite, indicating suppressed recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. This is further supported by improved photocurrent responses and reduced charge transfer resistance (as shown by EIS analysis), evidencing more efficient charge separation and faster interfacial electron transfer [96,97]. The role of CQDs differs between the two irradiation systems. Under visible light, CQDs act as photosensitizers, facilitating electron excitation and transfer to the TiO2 conduction band, while under UVC irradiation, their role primarily focuses on enhancing charge separation and reducing recombination. These results collectively demonstrate that CQD incorporation not only broadens light absorption but also significantly improves photocatalytic efficiency through dual mechanisms tailored to the light source [98].

To assess the durability and reusability of the PPCQD/TiO₂ photocatalyst composite, three-cycle experiments of PFOA photodegradation under visible light irradiation were conducted. After each run, filtration paper separated the photocatalyst composites from the PFOA solution. The composites were then rinsed with ultrapure water and ethanol three times to remove any residual contaminants. After washing, the photocatalyst composites were dried in an oven at 65 °C for 6 h and used again to degrade another freshly prepared PFOA solution.

The findings depicted in Fig. 10 indicate a decline of approximately 14 % in photodegradation efficiency during the second and third cycles of reuse. This decrease in efficiency can be attributed to several factors, including photo corrosion of the photocatalyst, accumulation of intermediates on the photocatalyst surface as observed in the FTIR results in Fig. S2, and reduced light penetration due to surface fouling. To mitigate this loss in efficiency, further purification of the photocatalyst using ethanol and ultrapure water is recommended to ensure the removal of residual intermediates and surface contaminants. Such measures could improve the long-term performance and stability of the PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalyst for repeated use in PFOA photodegradation.

This study focused on the photocatalytic oxidation of PFCAs, a significant concern in water contamination. Peapod biomass-based CQDs were synthesized hydrothermally and incorporated into TiO2 using the sol-gel method, resulting in a PPCQD/TiO2 photocatalyst composite. Characterization through FTIR, XRD, XPS, HRTEM, UV–Vis, PL, and other techniques confirmed the successful synthesis of materials with enhanced optical and photocatalytic properties. The photocatalytic degradation of PFOA-C8 and short-chain PFCAs (C3–C6) was investigated under UVC and visible light, with the rate constants for first-order kinetics calculated for PFCA photodegradation. The effect of radical scavengers and the presence of degradation intermediates and defluorination efficiencies were analyzed to understand the degradation pathway. The reusability of the photocatalyst composite was evaluated through three cycles of PFOA degradation under visible light irradiation. Results showed a significant improvement in the degradation efficiency of PFOA and short-chain PFCAs (C5–C6) when PPCQDs were incorporated into TiO2 compared to bare TiO2. However, the degradation efficiency of shorter-chain PFCAs (C3 and C4) decreased in mixed solutions, likely due to competitive adsorption. Intermediates formed during PFOA degradation under UVC and visible light confirmed the cleavage of longer-chain PFCAs into shorter-chain counterparts, supporting the proposed degradation mechanism. Furthermore, PPCQD/TiO2 demonstrated higher defluorination ratios, confirming its effectiveness in breaking down persistent perfluorinated pollutants.

Mona Nejatpour: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Bergüzar Yılmaz: Investigation. Burcu Ozden: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Sibel Barisci: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Meral Dükkancı: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.
